segments.text: Part 6E: Glycolysis Dr. Rizk

Glycolysis
Glucose transport into the cell is mediated by glucose transport proteins (GLUT-1 to GLUT-5)
GLUT-1 : RBCs, Brain, testicles and retina
GLUT-2 : liver, kidney, pancreas, intestine
GLUT-3 : Brain
GLUT-4 : Fatty tissue, skeletal and cardiac
GLUT-5 : (fructose) intestine and sperms
Know that glucose is transported into the cell by facilitated diffusion a form of passive transport facilitated by transport proteins.
NO ENERGY NEEDED

Glucose transport
Insulin stimulates glucose uptake by translocating GLUT- 4 protein to the plasma membrane (in muscles and adipose tissues)
Insulin
Glut-4
Insulin
Receptor
Glut-4
Glucose
[Speaker Notes] Change figure

18.1 What Are the Essential Features of Glycolysis?
Glycolysis is also called the Embden-Meyerhof (or Warburg) Pathway
Essentially all cells carry out glycolysis
Ten reactions – essentially the same in all cells but with different rates
Two phases:
First phase converts glucose to two glyceraldehyde-3-P
Second phase produces two pyruvates
Products are pyruvate, ATP and NADH
Four possible fates for pyruvate

Glycolysis
2 moles of ATP are utilized in glycolysis
4 moles of ATP are generated
The net production is 2 ATP
One mole of glucose →
2 moles of pyruvate
2 moles of NADH
Glucose
Glucose 6-P
ATP
Fructose 6-P
Fructose 1, 6 bis-P
ATP
2x G3-P
2x 1,3 BisP-Glycerate
2x 3 P-Glycerate
2x NADH
2x ATP

2x P-Enolpyruvate
2x Pyruvate
2x ATP

Glucose is kept in the cell by phosphorylation to glucose-6-phosphate
Figure 18.4 Glucose-6-P cannot cross the plasma membrane.

Rxn 1: Hexokinase or glucokinase
Glucose  G-6-P
Two different enzymes: same reaction
Hexokinase: most tissue
K m for glucose is 0.1 m M ; cell has 4 m M glucose (in the fed state)
So is normally active!
Glucokinase : Liver
( K m glucose = 10 m M ) only turns on when cell is rich in glucose
Preventing hyperglycemia after carbohydrate-rich meal

Glucokinase vs Hexokinase
Differences in the kinetics between hexokinase and glucokinase

Liver glucokinase
Glucokinase is only active at high intrahepatocyte [Glucose]
After a meal high is carbohydrates, the liver absorbs excess glucose
Glucokinase converts Glucose to G6P, trap it in the cell
Excess glucose is converted into glycogen for storage
Glycogen breakdown in the liver during fasting can supply tissue with glucose
Glucokinase Mutation  gestational diabetes
Glucose to G6P reaction

Reaction 2:  Phosphoglucoisomerase
Glucose-6-P to Fructose-6-P (isomerization reaction)
Why does this reaction occur?
next step (phosphorylation at C-1) would be tough for hemiacetal -OH, but easy for primary –OH
Next step also produces a symmetrical molecule (Fructose 1,6 BP), set up for cleavage into 2 similar 3-C sugars
Difficult to phosphorylate
Easier to phosphorylate

Reaction 3:  Phosphofructokinase
PFK is the committed step in glycolysis!
The second priming reaction of glycolysis
Rate Limiting step, means PFK is highly regulated:
ATP inhibits, AMP reverses inhibition
PFK increases activity when energy status is low
PFK decreases activity when energy status is high

Phosphofructokinase behaves cooperatively at high [ATP]
Figure 18.9  At high ATP, phosphofructokinase (PFK) behaves cooperatively and the activity plot is sigmoid.
The structure of phosphofructokinase

Rxn 4: Aldolase
A C 6 intermediate is cleaved to 2 C 3 s
Dihydroxyacetone phosphate (DHAP)
Glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate (G3P)

Aldolase 2X 3C products
The two products of aldolase (DHAP & G3P) are isomers
Only G3P is useful for the following steps
Instead of discarding DHAP, it is converted to G3P
Reaction is carried out by triose phosphate isomerase
C-C bond cleavage

The structure of triose phosphate isomerase
Triose phosphate isomerase with substrate
Enzyme forms a TIM barrel

Reaction 5:  Triose Phosphate Isomerase
Triose phosphate isomerase completes the first phase of glycolysis.  Each glucose has been converted to two molecules of glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate.

Reaction 5:  Triose Phosphate Isomerase
DHAP is converted to G-3-P
This reaction makes it possible for both products of the aldolase reaction to continue in glycolysis
The reaction involves an ene-diol mechanism
Glu 165 in the active site acts as a general base

Figure 18.13
Figure 18.13 A reaction mechanism for triose phosphate isomerase.  In the yeast enzyme, the catalytic residue is Glu 165 .
Remember this mechanism

Reaction 6: Glyceraldehyde-3-P Dehydrogenase
G-3-P is oxidized to 1,3-BPG
Energy yield from converting an aldehyde to a carboxylic acid is used to make 1,3-BPG and NADH
The mechanism involves covalent catalysis and a nicotinamide coenzyme, and it is good example of nicotinamide chemistry
This enzyme reaction is the site of action of arsenate – an anion analogous to phosphate

The glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate dehydrogenase reaction
This is a phosphorylation step that does not require ATP

Rxn 6: Glyceraldehyde-3-P Dehydrogenase
Figure 18.14 A mechanism for the glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate dehydrogenase reaction.  Reaction of an enzyme sulfhydryl with G3P forms a thiohemiacetal, which loses a hydride to NAD + to become a thioester. Phosphorolysis releases 1,3-bisphosphoglycerate.

Reaction 7: Phosphoglycerate Kinase
ATP synthesis from a high-energy phosphate
Phosphoglycerate kinase transfers a phosphoryl group from 1,3-bisphosphoglycerate to ADP to form an ATP.
This is referred to as “ substrate-level phosphorylation ”
This reaction “ pays off ” the ATP debt created by the priming reactions in the first phase
2,3-BPG (for hemoglobin) is made by circumventing the PGK reaction (Figure 18.15)

Reaction 7: Phosphoglycerate Kinase
Phosphoglycerate kinase transfers a phosphoryl group from 1,3-bisphosphoglycerate to ADP to form ATP.  This type of ATP-synthesizing reaction is referred to as a “ substrate-level phosphorylation ”

G3P-DH  is the site of action of arsenate
Arsenate is a substrate for the G3P-DH reaction, forming
1-arseno-3-phosphoglycerate.  This product breaks down to
3-phosphoglycerate, essentially bypassing the phosphoglycerate kinase reaction.  The result is that glycolysis in the presence of arsenate produces no net ATP.

2,3-BPG is made by reactions that detour around the phosphoglycerate kinase reaction
2,3-bisphosphoglycerate is an important regulator of hemoglobin
2,3-BPG is formed from 1,3-BPG by bisphosphoglycerate mutase
3-phosphoglycerate is then formed by 2,3-bisphosphoglycerate phosphatase
Most cells contain only a trace of 2,3-BPG, but erythrocytes typically contain 4-5 mM 2,3-BPG

2,3-BPG is made by reactions that detour around the phosphoglycerate kinase reaction
Figure 18.15 Formation and decomposition of 2,3-bisphosphglycerate.
Reaction 8: Phosphoglycerate Mutase

Reaction 9: Enolase
Conversion of 2-Phosphoglycerate to PEP
The enolase makes a high-energy phosphate in preparation for ATP synthesis in step 10

Reaction 10: Pyruvate Kinase
Conversion of PEP to pyruvate makes ATP
These two ATP (from one glucose) can be viewed as the "payoff" of glycolysis
Large, negative D G – indicating that this reaction is subject to regulation
PK is allosterically activated by AMP (low energy), and F-1,6-bisP (feedforward activation)
PK is allosterically inhibited by ATP (high energy) and acetyl-CoA (feedback inhibition)

Reaction 10: Pyruvate Kinase
The pyruvate kinase reaction converts PEP to pyruvate, driving synthesis of ATP.  Note that two ATP are produced here, since two PEP are formed from one glucose.  These two ATP are the “ payoff ” of glycolysis.

Pyruvate
Pyruvate is the end result of glycolysis
It is a partially oxidized α-keto-acid
It is a central molecule that has many fates depending on the conditions of the cell or organism

Glycolysis question
Under low oxygen conditions (hypoxia or exercise), the main reason pyruvate is converted to lactate is:
To maintain glucose levels in blood
To reverse the process of glycolysis
To maintain NAD level in cytoplasm
To prevent decrease in pH

The fate of pyruvate (mammals)

18.5 What Are the Metabolic Fates of NADH and Pyruvate Produced in Glycolysis?
Aerobic conditions , pyruvate proceeds through the tricarboxylic acid cycle, converted to acetyl-CoA
Anaerobic metabolism of pyruvate leads to lactate (in microorganisms and animals) or ethanol (in yeast)
A third pathway converts pyruvate into alanine (usually in the muscles):
Can be used in protein synthesis
Sent to the liver to convert back into glucose

Aerobic conditions
Pyruvate to acetyl-CoA
One C is lost as CO 2
Remaining acetyl group: attached to Co-A
Takes place in the mitochondria
Produces NADH
Acetyl-CoA uses:
Plug into TCA
Fatty acid
synthesis

Anaerobic conditions
Low oxygen (hypoxic) condition
Pyruvate is converted to lactate (mammals)
Pyruvate is converted to ethanol (yeast)
Low levels of acetyl-CoA production
Result for humans:
High levels of lactic acid  lactic acidosis

Glycolysis: NAD/NADH cycle

Pyruvate → lactate 2 NADH generated by glycolysis will be used to reduce pyruvate into lactate
IN THE ABSENCE OF OXYGEN
Know that the net result is 2 moles of ATP (glucose → lactate)
Glucose
2 ATP

Glycolysis question
Under low oxygen conditions (hypoxia or exercise), the main reason pyruvate is converted to lactate is:
To maintain glucose levels in blood
To reverse the process of glycolysis
To maintain NAD level in cytoplasm
To prevent decrease in pH

18.5 What Are the Metabolic Fates of NADH and Pyruvate Produced in Glycolysis?
NADH can be recycled via aerobic or anaerobic pathways
NADH represents energy - two possible fates:
If O 2 is available (aerobic conditions), NADH is oxidized in the electron transport pathway, making ATP in oxidative phosphorylation
In anaerobic conditions, NADH is oxidized by lactate dehydrogenase (LDH), providing additional NAD + for more glycolysis

18.5 What Are the Metabolic Fates of NADH and Pyruvate Produced in Glycolysis?
Figure 18.21 (a) Pyruvate reduction to ethanol in yeast provides a means for regenerating NAD + consumed in the glyceraldehyde-3-P dehydrogenase reaction. (Right) Fermentation at a bourbon distillery.  A “mash” of corn and other grains is fermented by yeast, producing ethanol and CO 2 , which can be seen bubbling to the surface.

18.5 What Are the Metabolic Fates of NADH and Pyruvate Produced in Glycolysis?
Figure 18.21 (b) In oxygen-depleted muscle, NAD + is regenerated in the lactate dehydrogenase reaction. Hibernating turtles, trapped beneath ice and lying in mud, become “anoxic” and convert glucose mainly to lactate.  Their shells release minerals to buffer the lactate throughout the period of hibernation.

Red blood cells: the Cori cycle
RBCs do not contain mitochondria
They rely entirely on glycolysis
Pyruvate is converted to lactate
Lactate is recycled by the liver into glucose
Cori Cycle

The Cori cycle
Overworked muscles produce lactate
Lactate is recycled by the liver through the Cori cycle
Liver is one of the few sites of gluconeogenesis

Pyruvate to alanine (alanine cycle)
Active muscles can convert pyruvate to alanine
Enzyme alanine transaminase (ALT)
Alanine is sent back to the liver
Liver can carryout the reverse reaction (alanine to pyruvate), then gluconeogenesis (glucose from pyruvate)
Glucose made in the liver is sent back to the muscle to replenish supply
This is known as the Alanine cycle

Inhibited by:

Summary of glycolysis
Investment stage: phosphorylation using ATP
PFK: rate-limiting step, highly regulated
Aldolase: only enzyme that breaks a C-C bond
TIM: DHAP to G3P (know the mechanism)
Payout stage:
G3PDH: produces NADH, makes high energy molecule using phosphorolysis (no ATP needed)
PK: favored reaction (enol to keto) producing ATP, highly regulated enzyme
Fates of pyruvate: Acetyl-CoA, lactate, ethanol or alanine
Know the Cori and alanine cycles
